From what I understand... I'm returning getjson value right away without any checking.

```
# Direct return

    'cached_until': self.py3.time_in(self.cache_timeout),
    'full_text': self.py3.safe_format(self.format, getjson)
}
```
... TO...
```
# Quick check before returning
if getjson:
    getjson = self.py3.safe_format(self.format, getjson)
else:
    getjson = self.py3.safe_format(self.format, None)

return {
    'cached_until': self.py3.time_in(self.cache_timeout),
    'full_text': getjson
}
```

Yeah, I think it makes sense to not fail here but instead rely on `AUTH_TYPE_MAP` (as it's a separate concern) to see if the resolver signature version is supported.

Also related to https://github.com/boto/botocore/pull/769#discussion_r51489723:

The problem we have is that the list of `signatureVersions` provided from resolving an endpoint can contain one or more signature version names. We need to somehow look in the list and choose the most appropriate signature version for Botocore. We know the following:

1. If the service is s3

   a.  if "s3" is available, then use "s3"
   b. use "s3v4" if available.

2. If "v4" is available use it.
3. ?  -- at this point how do we choose a signer? Just take element `[0]` of the list? Or should we iterate over the list of `signatureVersions` until we find a signer that is supported in `AUTH_TYPE_MAP`?

With the semantic versioning, the incompatible changes forcefully bump the major version, so you can just pin the requires to the major version and then be sure that unless there's a bug that was not picked up by ci/tests, it will never break your client code.

I'll try to get the release notes generated too (grouping bugs, features and apibreaking changes) since the preavious release, but in the meantime, just checking the entries where the major version changes in the changelog gives you an easy-to-check way to track those incompatible changes, and a grep/search for the bug id will give you the version, commit, author and link to the change that introduced the fix for it. Of course, if the developer did specify it in the commit message (that's harder to automate, as there's no way to know if the change is linked to an issue or if it's not, maybe we can force to open an issue for each pull request to make sure that at least there's a paper trail too for each change...)

raw_signature = excepts(StopIteration, first, lambda v: None)(log_entry['topics'])
